title: Incorrect behaviour in creating loop devices
body:
### Issue Description

Repeat of https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/17715 - adding a bit more detail so it gets picked up this time. I have confirmed the behaviour when 

- Running podman as root, and 
- Using `--privileged=true`, and 
- Adding `--cap-add SYS_ADMIN` explicitly

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Same repro instructions - you'll need a mountable image of some sort to work with, and you should ensure you have no `/dev/loop*` devices already present. In my case - I start with no `/dev/loopX` devices. 

If there are `/dev/loopX` devices extant but not in use (ie: visible via `ls /dev/loop*` but not visible in `losetup -la`) then the issue won't appear as the container will reuse the extant `loopX` device. The container has to both create a new `/dev/loopX` device, and attempt to use it for the bug to appear. 

```
sudo podman run --rm -it --privileged=true --cap-add SYS_ADMIN docker.io/library/debian:13 bash
```

In the container, make a test ext4 fs to mount
```
apt-get update
apt-get install e2fsprogs
dd if=/dev/zero of=/test_ext4 bs=1M count=0 seek=512 status=none
mkfs.ext4 -F  "/test_ext4"
```

Then - try to mount this ext4
```
mount -o loop,rw /test_ext4 /mnt
mount: /mnt: failed to setup loop device for /test_ext4.
```

Inside the container - there is no `/dev/loop0`. On my host system - `/dev/loop0` exists after the mount command is run. 

It seems the container can create the /dev/loop0 device, but can't pick it up after creation

### Describe the results you received

Can't see `/dev/loopX` after creation in the container - despite being able to create it from the container

### Describe the results you expected

Should see `/dev/loopX` after creation in the container - OR should see an error when trying to create the loop device if that is more appropriate behaviour. 

The issue is more that it can create it, but not read/use it. That's confusing. Should be all-or-nothing

### podman info output

Run via `sudo podman info` as this is for rootful containers

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

Stock Arch Linux install, nothing custom

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body: I have attempted this with `docker` as well and reproduced the same result - so this may not be at the container engine level but somewhere deeper.

With `--privileged`, we are not mounting `/dev` into the container, but making an individual mount for each item in `/dev/` on the host. So new devices not appearing in the container is, I would say, expected? A restart of the container ought to pick it up, though. I think manually mounting host `/dev/` into the container is also a solution?

> I think manually mounting host /dev/ into the container is also a solution?

I guess so? 

The behaviour as-is is still pretty unexpected (at least to me) - being able to create a `/dev` device and then not use it is mildly baffling. 

>  but making an individual mount for each item in /dev/ on the host. 

For my own understanding, why does it work like that? As opposed to the straight up `/dev` mount. When I'm `chroot`'ing stuff - I'd usually do a direct `/dev` mount - and that is effectively what I'm replicating in this case.

We do it this way to match Docker, which has had this behavior for at least the last 5 years and probably much longer than that. As to why they do it, I'm not entirely sure. There are some devices that need to be specific to the container, even when `/dev` from the host is mounted in (/dev/pts from memory, but more than I'm not remembering probably), but we could just mount those over /dev instead of recreating everything in there?

(Funnily enough, privileged rootless containers just mount /dev directly, because we don't have appropriate permissions to recreate the device nodes in question)

I suppose to give this some closure one way or the other - the behaviour to match docker makes sense in terms of compatibility. The behaviour in-and-of-itself does seem rather odd - but I'd imagine its not the thing you'd change willy-nilly. 

https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/27886 appears to be a fairly old description of the same issue, with which the conversation is deep, old and unresolved. 

The workaround appears to be to bind mount `/dev` - easy enough to do for my usecase at least.
